title: Branching & Merging
summary: Master Git branching and merging. Branches let you work on features, fixes, and experiments in isolation. Learn to create, switch, merge, and resolve conflicts — essential skills for any team workflow.

resources.title: Pro Git Book - Chapter 3: Git Branching
Learn Git Branching - Interactive Visual Tutorial
Git Merge vs Rebase - Atlassian Guide

questions.explanation: 'git branch <name>' creates a new branch pointer at the current commit but does not switch to it. Use 'git checkout <name>' or 'git switch <name>' to switch.

options.text: Creates and switches to a new branch
Creates a new branch without switching to it

questions.questionText: What happens during a merge conflict?
questions.explanation: A merge conflict occurs when Git cannot automatically merge because the same lines were changed differently in both branches. Git marks the conflicting sections and you must manually resolve them.

options.text: Git automatically picks the newer change
The merge is cancelled and changes are lost
Git marks conflicts in the file and you must manually resolve them
Git creates a new branch for the conflict

questions.explanation: A fast-forward merge happens when the target branch has not diverged from the source branch. Git simply moves the branch pointer forward, no merge commit is needed.

options.text: A merge that happens very quickly
A merge where the branch pointer is moved forward without creating a merge commit
A merge that skips conflict resolution
A merge that only includes the most recent commit

questions.questionText: How do you delete a branch that has been merged?
questions.explanation: 'git branch -d <name>' deletes a branch only if it has been fully merged. Use '-D' (capital) to force-delete an unmerged branch.

tasks.title: Branch, Merge, and Resolve Conflicts
tasks.description:
1. Start in your project from Day 8
2. Create and switch to a feature branch: `git switch -c feature/greeting`
3. Modify `app.sh` to add a greeting function
4. Commit the changes
5. Switch back to main: `git switch main`
6. Modify the same file differently on main and commit
7. Merge the feature branch: `git merge feature/greeting`
8. Resolve the merge conflict:
   - Open the file and look for `<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>` markers
   - Edit the file to keep the changes you want
   - Stage the resolved file: `git add app.sh`
   - Complete the merge: `git commit`
9. View the merge in the log: `git log --oneline --graph --all`
10. Delete the merged branch: `git branch -d feature/greeting`
hints: Use 'git switch -c branch-name' as a shortcut to create and switch in one command
After resolving conflicts, make sure to remove ALL conflict markers before staging

tasks.title: Multi-Branch Workflow Simulation
tasks.description:
1. Create three feature branches from main:
   - `git switch -c feature/auth`
   - `git switch main && git switch -c feature/logging`
   - `git switch main && git switch -c feature/config`
2. On each branch, create and commit a different file
3. Merge all three branches into main, one at a time
4. Observe the log graph: `git log --oneline --graph --all`
5. Experiment with `git merge --no-ff` to see how it creates merge commits even for fast-forward merges
6. List all branches: `git branch -a`
7. Clean up by deleting the merged branches
hints: The --no-ff flag forces a merge commit, which preserves the history of the feature branch
Use 'git branch -v' to see the last commit on each branch
